snippet: How I failed my System Design round – and how I learned to never freeze again. ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
subject: “Why wouldn’t you shard here?”
body:
How I failed my System Design round – and how I learned to never freeze again.

View in browser (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQbT5m_5PW6N1X8z6lZ3kXW1fLD8g20pm0KW3mYRK66qBsvcVldBTG2X8RhqVrPYv-81t1rFVDwt4G2CbcyBW4YXC_d3v7YBLW3cHJHT7dmK05W1ssqRg3PvsG3W8sg73-72Dq-rW1F86WJ6TtvX8W33yMcp52dxwBW8HJp525bg5GRW555yFj8ynlG2W1ck3mp1_pkL-W4zrJ0K3SsPrfW6kDrsQ4Qk0ZYVB-vqK4WSTWCW2y4y1d9brfkXW3w91283LLy9cW8qHpjn1bm1HlW1jxGzv4yFGHcW2Xgm3h7QjqFQW7JScjv6HZv5zW4jWn194VPvMnW79Tlnx4VCMT3W4_wlFD7jxmJWW3r3tc835kQq1W3PPFdj38kDFfW95y8Vp2vpfk4W4cjp4h67BJ15W1XhMM06sW8BsW2H-PNn7qmSZPW2yrmmP16KF_NW6Yk3BM98PpgmW3VKM2h7bj6RXW7ZpktL8GkQ_fW8YmJWX2wfptsW2vFz6Q7KkVwVd2X1CH04 )

Денис,

This week, we’re excited to welcome back our friend Alexandre Zajac. He's an Amazon SDE here to share one critical System Design Interview lesson that he learned the hard way.

Alexandre breaks down 3 strategies to build interview-ready judgment (the when, why, and what breaks) — plus a free visual mega-whiteboard covering 12 essential System Design patterns with diagrams, decision rules, and real examples from Netflix, Uber, Stripe, and Instagram.

🌼 ICYMI, Educative is also running a Spring Flash Sale — so for the next 48 hours you can cash in on 50% off (or more) (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQcM3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3llW5zQFdy39z5ZzW81Xqls3QPHV_W2zdJjh406Cb3W2_fp_Z67FlzTW5CWZMV19HxktW6V3cl17Pb3-gW7vzhW14XCQWfW8W7dPZ8nlG5RW1FkYvq1rMS_nN1Jc5Mr9SXD9W6k99C76qSYxmW3D8B8V8FrD_KVBjk2v3nhgpfW2l6jLQ8TVDmWW74Kqqc4M8SS5W91qxV11Dd_-3W5b3PcT4Tvvr_W8LW2xD5vkJskW3-NNPt2kpHcyW3KGYlP6mxDFSVpdCsk3CXH2WW2bh41Z5nDbkmW5PN7Vc3-6cmSVbW_N_3Sf3DdW88vPdf7rQj2bVWnx_52rwVYxW67J3nD4sY8RXW7dyG548g2Tlcf3Xf8js04 ) .

(This includes access to our most popular SD courses like Grokking the System Design Interview (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQ9-5m_5PW50kH_H6lZ3nxW7rJWbG5_CHbDW7QsR4c795R2BW8_0DRG6BZ6G9W2qgWSb3DLfV8W8KM1D76sYcK4W18Kf8866RZFZW3XRy-Z7JhpF4W90N9FD7-QtjhW7rxf0y5sjHG5W2-z5J16fssxSW8gvhcQ9chysXW2prRM18h6FSgW3WvnRD6fHPNXW4scBR-368dLJW3FNtjb49qz2mW5F6W__6KJPH7W2CRBRv99hc_wVn39B435T9w7W69nvPg2YDFDNN2TQxtzVB21ZW5wt5pR54ZWrWW2KC4_g5GCbDJW3sQTtT94zX0nW95Jh272Lp6WlW8ssZw62zVwRsW4vC5qn7bzlYCW2ZfsMc4CsrLzW5xt_4S1m2zm3W6FPB3N51rRqnW6zWxPB2gx5k4V5Sx622GhhDqW4BcX9X4y2sH4f1N3_9T04 ) and Agentic System Design (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQd23prCCW95jsWP6lZ3pFW123Y2z5K2_2SV8N0Fg5C54bXW3kXfw83sJ18cW1GSrV198GBpSW8FP0kZ4gwF8nW1Q9CCK36q6vBN1LDvBvFhG8kW17021D8xn7s7VD_p721w1D6LW31n-pX2YgFvHW2nBrq848PrCrMw1GWJSzs1MW2TgS_D3gK_NmW1jnsw_2sv-ZmW1YFm_T3j6djrW7RNKnb3vNzBFN7t152Spn-HcW3wBGyk7D_JkdW6NgvBp689tVSW1bDXBs5g3VTzW4K5ssw5vGzc5W6V3mqm5lXWGCV6ydhF8HCFbhVpNmdS8Vgcb6W794Wdc18dNKlW3mnLGl7VvxcDN5XM69JNMJrXW8gS4l44s7zqCW4RXLc56dl8SQW3H2PZr42PC0BdWc3Ts04 ) ).

Now over to Alexandre!

👋 Hi! It's Alex again.

When I started prepping for system design interviews, I felt confident.

I could draw the boxes. I knew the pattern names.

Load balancer, cache, message queue, database. Easy.

Then the interviewer asked: "Why wouldn't you share here?"

Silence.

I had memorized patterns. I hadn't learned tradeoffs. That gap cost me the round.

I've since rebuilt how I approach system design from the ground up. And I've distilled it into a visual mega-whiteboard that includes:

- 12 essential system design patterns with illustrated diagrams
- WHEN to use each (and when NOT to)
- WHY it works (the actual mechanics)
- TRADEOFFS that interviewers love to probe
- Real examples from Netflix, Uber, Stripe, and Instagram

I'll give you my core strategies below, but click here to grab the full whiteboard (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQcs3prCCW7Y8-PT6lZ3m9W5199xH6WFc6pW8g6Mpr5z8Tl7W5M9hKn7jj6dXW7Dq1jC6Q_Bm3W7G2lpK2RRCVMW3Kc_S52HCSTlW2glRDB5Z9H9bW55th3g8HqZwrW1vGRXd2BfB_jV1nr0n8qjmdkW40Qq6z9lHLVnW6vyv7P4tX6NQW7LX_Kr3Zh0dFW4QflQP85tH9FN3sY7dQ6JKZfN2JcyH3WbJflW6m9Fd06kZlmjW7RQtlg135CwrW7gF1DT4LcB6XW4jQkzC77lLcnW2TWSNS9fGqCFN3HR3VGYzGmGVsc58g53fpgfW6YZtjp76xcBrW5pKqrk6QGyR1W3cLDVJ1gjLVPf5gwzl-04 ) .

Now, let's get into it.

I was 40 minutes into a system design interview.

My architecture looked clean. Load balancer at the front. Cache layer. Sharded database. Message queue for async processing.

The interviewer nodded along. Then:

"Interesting. So why did you choose to shard the database here instead of just adding read replicas?"

I froze.

I had drawn sharding because... that's what you do at scale, right? I couldn't articulate the actual tradeoff. I didn't know when sharding was the wrong choice.

I was playing pattern bingo, not engineering.

That's when I realized: most system design resources teach you WHAT a pattern is. They skip the WHEN, WHY, and WHAT BREAKS.

Let me show you 3 strategies to fix that.

0. Learn tradeoffs first, patterns second

Do not memorize architecture diagrams.

When you study a new pattern (e.g., caching), don't just learn "cache speeds up reads." Learn:

-

When does caching HURT? (Write-heavy workloads, cache invalidation nightmares)

-

What breaks? (Stale data, cold start problems, memory costs)

-

What's the alternative? (Read replicas, query optimization, CDN for static assets)

This is building your decision-making framework.

How to do it: Go to Educative's "Grokking the System Design Interview" (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQbg5m_5PW5BWr2F6lZ3nBVLlm2X20bstNW6Rr6S359y59GW387lpG8_zfGNW7xn7hQ8cqjYcW6_RJpz47yp5vW8BSdWt6389B-W3nmjSP79dTKkW3FkKbH1dT0bSW7jwdkH6Spn6ZW6kPvv41kWZyxW2pqQWq3lRRTzW63PWN13P430zVlS1kd5bt4fbW7zLPnt1Nmk1xW2QcNds2M4mGdW1k0F9w3C2L7bW1XRp9P4KdggSW90XSy043vYvsW92GN-b2yd-v8N5FDzjhwS_RKN114NCL1wVPGW109hcl78zG66N3vtk61xYrp_N5DfHCXCqrrRW2ccgkL48BRJHW35KCpF1R0tf4W1ffwXR7xswJ9V_Pv4-4StWRgN1MQ3KxzcZxyW1l1TPg5z9pfQW3LmRpV8Jz74wVBBwtP13N14DW5kcWhM8Mfp3-W3rx82R5xf9jqdyj_JT04 ) course. For each pattern, don't just read the "how it works" section. Focus on the "when to use" and "limitations" sections. That's where the interview gold is.

Most candidates can explain caching. Few can explain when caching is the wrong choice. Be the second type.

1. Build your "why" library, not your pattern count

You memorized 15 patterns. Bravo. Here's your medal. 🏅

But that doesn't matter if you can't justify your decisions under pressure.

There are ~12 core patterns that appear in 90% of system design interviews. For each one, you need to internalize:

-

The problem it solves

-

The tradeoff it introduces

-

A real-world example of how it's actually implemented

My method: For each pattern, I'd memorize one killer example with specific details.

Not "Netflix uses caching."

But "Twitter caches timelines in Redis. When you refresh, you're hitting cache, not querying the social graph. That's why timeline loads take ~50ms instead of 500ms. But it means your timeline can be stale for a few seconds after someone posts."

See the difference? One is a fact. The other is a tradeoff story.

The whiteboard I made has these for all 12 patterns. You can grab it below.

2. Practice the "why not" game out loud

Your diagram is only half the interview. The other half is defending it.

Here's a game I played that leveled me up fast:

After drawing any architecture, I'd ask myself "why not" for every component:

-

"I added a load balancer." → "Why not just DNS round-robin?"

-

"I'm using Redis for caching." → "Why not just use database query caching?"

-

"I'm sharding by user ID." → "Why not by geography? Why not by time?"

If I couldn't answer, I'd go research until I could. The best part is that you can do this with AI as well!

The rubber duck interview: Explain your architecture out loud before the interview. "Okay, this is a read-heavy system with global users, which means I need caching at the edge. I'll use a CDN for static assets, but for dynamic data, I'll need a distributed cache. Redis Cluster makes sense here because..."

Use mock platforms: Schedule mock interviews (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQd23prCCW95jsWP6lZ3lXW86hXqY1xD7CQW6MT5W010_jFRW5nlnYp5S-bKnN7Xh_VLRPf5MW1VGXKd6GHgZ4W4r0Wsh8ZT4pQW60f_TB1p9tMzW9fkwyT933wH2W35KyFT6046_WW7N2Hp-88M-9HW2tM2kD7r_9yZVC0x3682g787W5d4Sf95qBc1KW7Ft2KW8KjzZXW5Vw83m7lwngDW6JfpCN2GCKF8W7t314G7k6RZlN5s04-Plb8llW8slQYk8bSmg2W1jtVhs5dWdJCVnzQ6y8NNNyVW1H7Fh81JyJFgW8z7q-t6jG5QzVl0L0-2kKJmpW75FD8X8qkqPrW3jd91Z6vGMsyW6n2TZL3Tc3_YW99bD2j8mkH88N82TjQYJSsqbW51Rvcz3LqZlYf3DljJl04 ) . Practice with a real person. Nothing matches the pressure of someone asking "but why?" three times in a row.

If you're targeting senior roles, you need a plan:

Weeks 1-2 (Foundations): Master the top 6 patterns. Load Balancing, Caching, Sharding, Replication, Message Queues, CDN. These appear in literally every interview. For each one, know the tradeoffs cold.

Weeks 3-4 (Advanced patterns): Add Rate Limiting, Circuit Breaker, API Gateway, Service Discovery, CQRS, Saga Pattern. These separate senior from mid-level answers. There are obviously many more, but that should get you leveled up.

Weeks 5-6 (Real systems): Study how actual companies implement these patterns. Netflix's multi-layer load balancing. Instagram's user ID sharding. Uber's Kafka-based event architecture.

Weeks 7-8 (Mocks and company-specific): Practice under timed conditions. Focus on your target companies' known questions. Defend every decision out loud.

This framework works. It's how I stopped freezing on "why" questions.

But building it from scratch, finding the right examples, mapping tradeoffs for each pattern, and creating visual references, took time.

A lot of time.

The market doesn't wait.

That's why I packaged everything into my system design whiteboard. It's the visual reference I wish I had.

👉 Access it here for free (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQcs3prCCW7Y8-PT6lZ3m9W5199xH6WFc6pW8g6Mpr5z8Tl7W5M9hKn7jj6dXW7Dq1jC6Q_Bm3W7G2lpK2RRCVMW3Kc_S52HCSTlW2glRDB5Z9H9bW55th3g8HqZwrW1vGRXd2BfB_jV1nr0n8qjmdkW40Qq6z9lHLVnW6vyv7P4tX6NQW7LX_Kr3Zh0dFW4QflQP85tH9FN3sY7dQ6JKZfN2JcyH3WbJflW6m9Fd06kZlmjW7RQtlg135CwrW7gF1DT4LcB6XW4jQkzC77lLcnW2TWSNS9fGqCFN3HR3VGYzGmGVsc58g53fpgfW6YZtjp76xcBrW5pKqrk6QGyR1W3cLDVJ1gjLVPf5gwzl-04 ) : Get the 12-pattern breakdown with illustrated diagrams, tradeoff boxes, real-world examples, and pro tips.

Your challenge this week isn't to memorize 12 patterns.

It's to master ONE pattern so well that you can explain the tradeoffs in your sleep.

Then go get the map that shows you the other 11.

Then, repeated practice defending your decisions.

You got this!

If you found this helpful, join 50,001+ curious engineers in my free weekly newsletter (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQcs3prCCW7Y8-PT6lZ3q5W5RB7gN80Nf-_W6k2Jld2Dy-DyW48F4684FV5SbW75Z95M95J0SWW3NDBSD8-z8rFW506nm_1HbTDyW7T35ZB5SPSDBW4mf6R04hs5SMW5sMv3r5JKbhgMMzzTllb4NXW68Krrp1s4cpXW3HNnM24L3c1NVd-WNX19TF8kW8ldxqK41XSnbW2CX8rK1vjl0DW4hf3vb50CBtqW71Vrrl47pTpmN3rs-0YMpPwWVY1f3Q1nC0BFW8XXq4j4KZ6y7V9336p2fHsjWN21nhJzyCNSGV-KYyc7V_3jFW2w1Pl327mgH6W1_JG9k2zfhw9W5r1TJg6SLzBPf4Pbq6n04 ) and follow my daily posts on LinkedIn (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsqVS79qnWmW7JDSGn94LM77W4qM5Xr5M7Pc4N4dVQcM3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3lHVkzsBr7z2sF9W2bRsHx4wLyrMW8fZxW42MC6tQW3HPyY46fFqxpW3WL4RJ19q-ffW4jRg047kLPNgVC_wLf7qkn7qW10QJhM9j61fwMjZwrzhS6gFN6kYBR7RjR2VVHQ6Gp7QYR1MN7D7_jRddWQRW7kT7N_27gCp9MFG8sXGWxRDN1lKnXK2p1DKW4sLZft6HrgrXW1BKd0C55GStwW3_mb0791DlXXW1tmyPZ24k890W4FbF6j52jlzWN68cSN3MsNWYW8LFZC428xQjQVzfNM58Hk2FqVpLP_t6tGVTRVcYqcy4x6HqxW7N_nlT270v2vW1WZySW3PRC9sW5-rSMB5SmPbHf8yPTnj04 ) .

Until next time, thank you!

Alexandre Zajac

Software Engineering and Data Science | Amazon

Institut Polytechnique de Paris
